FOREWORD
This research publication on Bail and Recognizance is one of a
series in a comprehensive study of the Code of Practice in Criminal
Cases directed by the 1958 General Assembly in Senate Bill No. 287. *
Preparation of initial publications in this series has been primarily for
the use of the members of the Legislative Research Commission and the
Criminal Code Committee established to advise and assist the commis-
sion " for the purpose of improving criminal practice and procedure in
the courts of the Commonwealth . " Publication in the present form is
for immediate distribution to members of the General Assembly and
others particularly interested in revision of criminal procedure .
Most of the publications in the series consist of a monograph on
the subject and a study of the pertinent code sections together with pro-
posals for revision . All code section studies and proposals are the joint
product of a study group consisting of the Honorable Rodes K. Myers ,
Professor A. C. Russell , Professor Roy Moreland , Reviser of Statutes
Dee A. Akers , and members of the Statute Revision section of the com-
mission's staff . The monographs are prepared by members of the staff .
The form in which the material is presented is that most useful to the
commission and the committee in appraising the work of the study group
in terms of existing law .
Initial examination of the code sections relating to Bail and Re-
cognizance was made by Mr. Moreland . Initial preparation of the
monograph on this subject was accomplished by W. Owen Keller of the
commission staff .
Frankfort , Kentucky
September , 1959

entries.ocr_text:
INTRODUCTION
Historical
The institution of bail is an ancient one , and , it has been said , is
as old as the English law itself . The definitive structure of the process
seems to have been first codified in 1275 during the reign of Edward I , in
the Statutes of Westminster . Development of the practice became so well
established that the English Bill of Rights of 1689 provided that " excessive
bail ought not to be required . " The English system of bail has not been
greatly changed since that time .
With some modification , the English concept of bail has been
adopted in the United States . The federal constitution , in the eighth amend-
ment , adopted in 1791 , reads " Excessive bail shall not be required , nor
excessive fines imposed , nor cruel and unusual punishment inflicted . " The
Federal Judiciary Acts of 1789 had guaranteed bail as a matter of right ,
and this guarantee is continued in Rule 46 of the Federal Rules of Criminal
Practice . Section sixteen of the Constitution of Kentucky provides that
"All prisoners shall be bailable by sufficient securities , unless for capital
offenses when the proof is evident or the presumption great . " This pro-
vision had been in the three earlier constitutions . The " excessive bail "
ban is expressed in Section 17 of the Kentucky constitution .
11
The word " bail " is used in American law as both a verb and a
noun , and as a noun it is sometimes used as a synonym for "bond , some-
times as a reference to the person who secures the bond . In the bond the
defendant is referred to as the principal , his bail as surety . The Court of
Appeals of Kentucky , in construing this provision of the criminal code
prescribing proceedings upon forfeiture , uses the following language :
" The word ' bail ' as there in used obviously signifies the persons who had
become security for the appearance of the accused in court . " 2 The court
also has said that bail is contract between the sureties and the state , and
upon failure of performing the convenants therein , the penalties provided
will be enforced . The Kentucky criminal code provides , " The bail shall
be residents of the Commonwealth , " 3 indicating that only natural persons
may be taken as bail . The Kentucky constitution however uses the term ,
" sufficient securities " which would seem to justify the taking of corporate
surety .

entries.ocr_text:
The primary purpose of bail is to allow an accused liberty of his
person pending his trial to determine his guilt or innocence , and at the
same time provide restraint that will secure his appearance at the proper
time . In ancient times many prisons lacked security , and the sheriff ,
into whose custody the accused was given , could not personally care for
all defendants awaiting trial . The prisoner was delivered , or " bailed , "
to his friends by the custodian under such conditions as would make his
presence available on the trial date . The security given , and the amount
thereof , was the responsibility of the sheriff as the custodial official .
benefits accruing were two - fold : first , the accused was allowed personal
freedom until found guilty of crime ; and second , the state was saved the
expense incident to incarceration .
The
In misdemeanor cases where the accused was known and his ties
in the community were more apt to be established , he was often released
on his own recognizance ; that is , he was " recognized " by the court , and
upon his own personal pledge was released until the date of trial . The
recognizance , while oral , was a sworn agreement , and the property of the
defendant was effectively pledged . This has remained a process of con-
venience and is used by all courts in the exercise of reasonable discretion .
Modern Application
With the modern - day increase in crime and the broadening of
judicial structure , it has become necessary for the courts to exercise
more stringent control over bail , and it thus has become primarily a
judicial function , rather than an administrative one . Courts are qualified
to determine the character of the crime charged , as well as the appropriate
amount of bail and the qualification of sureties . Unfortunately this has
often become a routine matter with little attention being given to it . In
most cases there is no specially trained personnel available to make a
study of the accused , his criminal and social background , his ability to
make bond , or to get the background of the crime itself . A " pre -bond "
investigation , such as that made in federal courts before sentencing , is of
particular value at this point in criminal proceedings .
In magistrate's courts which have only misdemeanor jurisdiction ,
the fixing of bail presents no great problem . Since the residence of the
accused is generally local , the court knows the background of the defendant
and can judge conditions more accurately than possible in courts of more
general jurisdiction . As a result , a recognizance is often sufficient to
guaranty the presence of the defendant .
In cases involving a non - resident , both the court and the defendant
encounter special difficulties . The state has the right to bring the charges
to trial , and the defendant a right to be heard . The defendant is unknown

with his home takes time and money , and the transportation of his pro-
spective bail to the jurisdiction of the court is more expensive than the
normal fine imposed would be , if found guilty .
In traffic violations , particularly , there can be a lack of justice .
The motorist is anxious to be on his way , and will post bail equivalent to
a fine , to be forfeited by his non - appearance for trial . This can possibly
be equitable to both sides , but has frequently resulted in " speed traps "
where the motorist's cash bond is forfeited for the benefit of the court
and its officers . The 1950 amendment to the Criminal Code 4 providing for
a citation also offers some degree of relief in this matter .
Although this monograph is concerned primarily with bail as con-
templated in criminal procedure , bail is also a part of civil procedure .
In form , the two concepts of bail are similar in many respects , differing
mostly in consequences . In either case , the principal is viewed as being
released from the custody of the law and placed in custody of a keeper of
his own selection . Qualification , exoneration and discharge are substant-
ially the same in both kinds of bail . The primary distinction is that pay-
ment of the indebtedness by the bail in a civil case discharges the entire
obligation of the principal to his creditors , while payment by the bail in
criminal cases , although it discharges the bail , does not discharge the
obligation of the principal to appear in court to answer to the charge . In
this jurisdiction , provisions as to criminal bail are a part of the Criminal
Code , 5 while those as to civil bail are statutory.6
The bail bond must also be distinguished from a peace bond which
serves a different purpose , but sometimes is coupled with bail . The
peace bond is an instrument of preventive justice , whereas a bail bond
is part of a procedure to insure appearance in court . The peace bond has
a probational aspect which is not present in a simple bail bond . The peace
bond is , however , in the nature of a bail bond , 7 and its terms may be
incorporated in the terms of bail bonds .

without regard to the seriousness or degree of the offense , or the nature

the seriousness of the offense and the degree of punishment were taken
into consideration.8 This discretion has been somewhat limited at both
ends , inasmuch as now most jurisdictions have provisions in their consti-
tutions guarantying and defining the right of bail in certain kinds of offenses .
The Kentucky constitution states the general rule : " All prisoners shall be
bailable by sufficient securities , unless for capital offenses , when the
proof is evident or the presumption great . " 9
The exception with regard to capital cases is well stated in a
Nevada case : " Where it cannot be known until after conviction whether
death will be the punishment , the question in legal contemplation on the
application for bail , is whether the offense is of a character that may be
punished capitally . In capital cases , it is generally held that constitutional
exceptions , such as ours , merely deprive the prisoner of his right to de-
mand bail guarantied under all cases , and leave with the court the discre-
tionary power vested in it at common law ; therefore the court may grant
bail where peculiar circumstances appear , or where the court , in the
exercise of sound judicial discretion , determines that the proof is not
evident and the presumption is not sufficiently great . " 10 In Kentucky the
presumption of innocence puts on the Commonwealth the burden of showing
that the proof is evident or the presumption is great.11
The English rule is deserving of notice at this point . In England ,
crimes , for the purposes of bail , have been divided by statute into two
classes : ( 1 ) felonies and certain specified misdemeanors , and ( 2 ) all other
misdemeanors . With respect to the first , the justices have a discretion.
as to whether they shall admit to bail or not . With respect to the second
12class , admission to bail is demandable as a matter of right .
One reason for the common law rule of bailability for capital
cases arises from the fact that the English common law recognized more
than one hundred offenses for which the punishment was capital . Offenses
now recognized as petit larceny , minor theft and even trespasses invoked
a penalty of death . Amelioration of these stringent penalties developed as
it became more and more apparent that the punishment should fit the crime .
As statutory capital offenses became limited to major crimes , bail for
such offenses became less available .

The number of capital offenses is now relatively limited , with
murder heading the list . Rape , armed robbery , kidnapping and treason
probably follow in that order of frequency , and all are now bailable in
the sound discretion of the court . The power of the Supreme Court of the
United States to admit a person to bail on a charge of high treason has
been exercised at an early date , although the circumstances favoring the
application which will induce the court to grant bail to one charge with high
treason must be very strong . 13
Many states have abolished capital punishment . In most of these
states all offenses are bailable . However , it has been said that " The fact
that the penalty which may be imposed may be , instead of death , imprison-
ment for life or for a term of years , has been held not to change the
captial character of the offense , so as to entitle a person charged there-
with to bail , under a constitutional provision , that all persons shall be
bailable except for capital offenses . " 14
The writ of habeas corpus is the device most often used to test
the bailability of an offense , although the writ is generally used in crimi-
nal cases only where the judgment is challenged as void . The early
English practice seems to have been that no hearing could be had in ex-
culpation where the prisoner was under indictment for a capital crime ,
and no witnesses could be heard . Modern practice , however , based upon
constitutional and statutory provisions , makes habeas corpus the proper
vehicle to use in obtaining the release of a prisoner upon bail , whether the
offense be capital or otherwise . Under the writ the court may hear not
only the demand for bail , but also the contention that the bail is excessive .
Some offenses are bailable after conviction upon appeal , while
others are not . The Kentucky criminal code 15 specifies the various offenses
in this jurisdiction that are not bailable after conviction . The distinction
is generally related to the degree of punishment or the seriousness of the
offense . This is in line with other states which set out by statute the
offenses not bailable after conviction .
EXECUTION OF BAIL
Admission to Bail
The machinery for the execution of bail is set up by statute or
code in most of the states . In federal procedure it is found in the crimi-
nal rules.16 In Kentucky it is contained chiefly in the Criminal Code of

Practice . Both the federal rules and the Kentucky criminal code imple-
ment the guaranties of bail set forth in the federal and state constitutions ,
both instruments recognizing bail as a matter of right . This right vests
important powers in the courts .
The Kentucky criminal code defines bail and the manner of exe-
cuting it to a greater degree than the federal rules or the provisions of
most of the other states . The code defines admission to bail as " an order
from a competent court or magistrate , that the defendant be discharged
from actual custody on bail . " 17 There are in general four situations in
which there can be admission to bail : ( 1 ) upon arrest with a warrant ;
( 2) after being held by examining court ; ( 3 ) after indictment ; and ( 4 ) after
conviction pending appeal .
Where warrants of arrest are issued , the amount of bail is gener-
ally fixed by the court issuing the warrant . The bail may be taken by the
sheriff or the coroner in his absence and the trial date fixed in the warrant.18
The sheriff is liable on his bond for the taking of bail . The authority to
take bail under such circumstances is a unique extension of judicial author-
ity to an arresting officer .
Bail is taken by the examining court for future appearance there ,
or is fixed by it to appear before a grand jury . An oral request may be
made for bail when arrest is made without a warrant or when no amount
is fixed in the warrant . However , where the defendant has been committed
to jail , a written petition is required to make application for bail before
a magistrate or the judge of the circuit court during vacation.19
After the indictment , bail is fixed by the circuit court , where a
much greater degree of caution can be exercised , inasmuch as the nature
of the offense has been definitely established and the characteristics of
the defendant are better known to the court .
After conviction , the defendant may be admitted to bail except in
capital cases , manslaughter , where the penalty exceeds five years im-
prisonment , and certain crimes of violence . 20 Bail after conviction must
be executed in the trial court before a record of appeal is filed in the Court
of Appeals . If bond is executed after the record is filed with the Court of
Appeals , it must be executed before the Clerk of the Court of Appeals .
The trial judge , however , must fix the sum of the bond , by an order which
is made part of the judgment .

There are some miscellaneous provisions concerning bail in
special or temporary cases . During periods of adjournment of examining
courts , a defendant may give bail in bailable cases , or he may employ
the sheriff as a special guard . During his trial in circuit court he may
remain on bail if bailable , or may be admitted to bail in the discretion of
the court .
The matter of bail arises incidentally to other stages of arraign-
ment and trial . Where a motion to set aside an indictment is granted , the
court makes an order directing the submission of the case to another jury
and the liability of bail is for the appearance of the defendant to answer a
new indictment . 21 The same procedure applies where there is an arrest
of judgment22 and where an indictment has been quashed and the jury dis-
charged . Where a defendant is on bail and has failed to appear , or where
the court is satisfied that bail is dead or insufficient , the court may enter
an order for arrest and recommitment and the defendant may again be
admitted to bail in the amount fixed by the court . 24
23
Amount of Bail
One of the objects of a preliminary hearing is to determine the
amount of bail to be fixed if the defendant is held . Within limits , the
question of the amount rests with the court in the exercise of its discretion .
In the interest of this discretion an examination of the bases for fixing the
amounts should be useful to the court and helpful in the administration of
justice .
Bearing in mind that the primary purpose of bail is to secure the
person of the accused to appear at his trial , the nature ofthe crime be-
comes the first important matter to consider . In cases such as larceny ,
embezzlement , and the like , the scale of punishment is within narrow
limits . In sex offenses the scope widens , and in homicide the punishment
ranges from a few days in jail or a fine for involuntary manslaughter to
the death penalty if the crime is found to be murder . The burden upon the
examining court is , therefore , a great one .
Considerable testimony must be heard to establish the major
elements of the charged offense and often the state will offer as little
testimony as possible in order not to disclose all its evidence . In a mat-
ter of simple theft , for example , it is not difficult for the court to arrive

the bail in each case could be small , yet cumulatively the amount could
be so great as to be impossible to meet , and " excessive bail " would result .
On the other hand , a substantial bond might be ineffective , where three
defendants illegally acquire a sum of $ 25,000 , a bond of $ 5,000 would be
so inadequate as to constitute an open invitation to " jump bond . "
The apparent guilt of one charged with crime also is a factor in
settling upon the amount of bond . If a case is " open and shut ' a court
would take special care that a proper bond be given , which would doubtless
be greater than required in a borderline case , or where there might be
little connection shown to the charged crime .
The character and criminal record of an accused are proper sub-
jects of inquiry in fixing the amount of bail . A youth in trouble for the
first time , and with a good background and family ties , would not require
a bond as large as a formerly convicted felon , even though the offenses
committed be the same .
Financial status -- --the ability to make bond is also a factor .
A man whose family is of limited means could be held in prison until trial ,
where an identical amount of bail would be easy for another to pledge .
Failure to take notice of financial conditions could easily make bail exces-
sive for a poor man whose presence could be guaranteed with smaller bail .
Courts have recognized the health of an accused as being important
in setting the amount of bail . A person suffering from tuberculosis or a
malignant disease often would have his life seriously endangered by con-
finement , and would constitute a menace to the health of others confined .
Few prisons have anthing approaching hospital facilities , and a physician
cannot treat properly a patient in prison . Everything considered , such a
prisoner could be bailed with safety by a smaller amount , and both the
prisoner and the state would benefit .
The foregoing is directed to bail for appearance at the examining
trial . At the later stages the problem is simpler , since many of the
factors present at the examining trial have been resolved and enough facts
are before the court to enable it to adjust the amount to the circumstances
of fact and to applicable statutory provisions .
It is in capital cases that fixing the amount of bail sees its widest
variance . The Kentucky constitution permits denial of bail only in capital
cases " when the proof is evident and the presumption great . " Here , of
course , the degree of the offense is a matter of particular importance in
determining the amount of bail . In a homicide case , if it is deemed
murder by the examining court , bond can be denied . The same applies

Section 16 of the Kentucky constitution : " In capital cases , therefore , the
matter of bail or no bail is in the sound discretion of the court . 1125
The notoriety given a case often plays an important part in settling
upon the amount of bail to be fixed . In a highly publicized case charging
sedition , the Kentucky Court of Appeals approved the fixing of a $ 40,000
bond by the circuit court . In a New York case , bail was reduced from
27$ 50,000 to $ 15,000 on a charge of " fixing " athletic contests .
26
Taking of Bail
Although admission to bail is primarily a judicial function , the
actual taking of the surety is something less . The code is explicit about
this step : " The taking of bail consists in the acceptance by a competent
court , magistrate or officer , of the undertaking of sufficient bail for the
appearance of the defendant according to the terms of the undertaking , or
128that the bail will pay to the Commonwealth the specified sum . The
code contains a section limiting the officers who can take bail to sheriffs ,
29deputy sheriffs , and coroners , where the office of sheriff is vacant .
However , a constable is also authorized to take bail under a warrant of
arrest . 30
Qualification of Bail
The court must be satisfied with the qualifications of the sureties
offered as bail . Most jurisdictions impose certain standards by statute as
a framework for the guidance of the court which are specific in nature .
Except in most metropolitan areas , the surety offered on a bond is a person
who is known to the court , or who can satisfy the court of his solvency and
ability to produce the defendant upon trial .

The qualifications for bail in Kentucky are in general regulated
by the criminal code . " The bail shall be residents of the Commonwealth ,
owners of visible property , over and above that exempt from execution ,
to the value of the sum in which bail is required , and shall be worth that
1131amount after the payment of their debts and liabilities . Any person
offered as bail may be examined on oath as to his qualifications and any
statement of financial status may be reduced to writing and sworn to , in
the discretion of the authorized officer to take the bail ; other proof may
likewise be taken by the officer . 32
Sufficiency of Bail
The determination of the sufficiency of bail has been held to be
ministerial , rather than judicial . 33 After the court has discharged the
judicial function of determining whether the offense charged is bailable ,
and has fixed the amount of bail , the clerk may , in his ministerial capac-
ity , determine the sufficiency of the sureties offered , and may accept
and approve the bail when directed by the judge to do so . In Kentucky
more than one person being offered as bail can be accepted if they are
sufficient in the aggregate .
34
It is here that a weakness exists in the system of bail . The surety
may , at the time of executing bond , own ample property above his legal
exemptions , and the bail is sufficient so long as he owns the property .
There are no restrictions on the mortgaging or sale of property pledged
as bail , at least in the Commonwealth . The only restriction applies at
the time of making bail . There is nothing to prevent a maximum mortgage
from being executed after pledging property as bail . If sold , no lien at-
taches as to the bail for which it pledged , and no lis pendens or other
restraint exists as to the title . John Doe , for example , may have real
estate valued at fifty thousand dollars above his exemptions , which property
is pledged as bail . He sells the property , and dissipates the proceeds .
There is no lien on the property sold , and now he possesses no property
subject to execution . The bail has become a nullity .

Courts generally have inherent powers to take custody of a de-
fendant in situations where bail later becomes insufficient . Such power
is prescribed by the code . 35 The matter must be brought to the court's
attention , presumably by counsel for the state . Thus is raised the
question whether a transfer of property pledged as bail is a fraudulent
conveyance under KRS 378.010 , so as to give the state a right of action
thereon .
Bail Bond
The form of the bail bond is set out in the criminal code . The
undertaking is substantially that the accused will at all times render him-
self amendable to the orders and process of the court , and on failure will36pay a fixed amount to the Commonwealth .
The code is liberal where irregularities of small substance are
present in the making of bail . It provides :
No bail bond or bail recognizance shall be deemed
to be invalid by reason of any variance between its stipu-
lations and the provisions of this Code , nor by the failure
of the magistrate or officer to transmit or deliver the
same at the times herein provided , nor by any other ir-
regularity , provided it be made to appear that the defend-
ant was legally in custody , charged with a public offense ,
and that he was discharged by reason of the giving of the
bond or recognizance , and provided it can be ascertained
from the bond or recognizance that the bail undertook
that the defendant should appear before a magistrate for
the examination of the charge , or before a court for the
trial thereof . If no day be fixed for such appearance , or
an impossible day , or a day in vacation , the bond of
recognizance , if for his appearance before a magistrate ,
shall be considered as binding the defendant so to appear
and surrender himself into custody , for an examination
of the charge , within twenty days from the time the bond
or recognizance was given ; and if for his appearance in
a court for trial , shall be considered as binding the de-
fendant so to appear and surrender himself into custody ,
on the first day of the next term of the court , which shall
commence more than ten days after the time when the
bond or recognizance is given . " 37

Professional Bondsmen
The complexities of modern jurisprudence have had great impact
upon the matter of bail . In earlier days , and presently in rural counties
of the Commonwealth , the court has personal knowledge of the security ,
and who is acceptable . In fact , as already stated , the criminal code makes
implicit the taking of " residents " as bail . Although the deposit of money
in lieu of bail is provided to secure the appearance of the defendant at the
examining trial , presumably the cash deposit can be retained as bail
38throughout the trial of the case .
The Kentucky constitution , however , provides that " all prisoners
shall be bailable by sufficient securities " ( Emphasis the writer's ) . 39 This
brings up an aspect of bail not heretofore mentioned , that of the profes-
sional bondsman . This aspect completely departs from the ancient theory
of bail where a friend assumes control of an accused person to guaranty
his appearance . The professional bondsman transacts his business almost
entirely in metropolitan areas and maintains his place of business in the
shadow of the courthouse , offering security underwritten by a corporate
entity in most cases . His services are utilized to a great extent by the
stranger or the friendless , who is accused of crime . His business is closely
associated with lawyers in the criminal practice . If properly administered ,
this procedure could be effective , although prohibitive in cost to an indigent
defendant accused of even a small crime .
The problem has become a serious one , and because of the nature
of such business it is regulated in some states under the police power of
governing authorities . Many of the larger cities have sought to establish
some control by ordinance . Kentucky has no statutes regulating bondsmen ,
but the City of Louisville has an ordinance providing a degree of regulation.
of professional bondsmen .
While the number of states now having statutes or regulations
governing the licensing of professional bondsmen has not been determined ,
there are many . The right to legislate on regulating and licensing the
business of the professional bondsman has been challenged on constitutional
grounds and in all such cases the constitutionality of the statute or regula -
tion has been upheld .
In California , the professional bondsman law is a part of the
insurance code , and is administered by the insurance commissioner .
The California court held that " The bail bond business is such a business
as is subject to reasonable regulation under the police power of the

Often the surety , a professional bondsman , is allowed

be worthless . One instance of worthless security is cited

valued at $ 25,000 , but covered by a $ 11,500 mortgage . It
is evident that where the security is extremely inadequate

feiture and will give no aid to the court in its effort to find
the accused . Such laxity on the part of the court in scru-

the ultimate defeat of the purpose of bail administration . 1143
Deposit of Money in Lieu of Bail
The acceptance of a money deposit in lieu of bail is regarded as
an optional matter in many jurisdictions , being left to the sound discretion.
of the court . In some jurisdictions it is not acceptable in all cases , and
in others it is regulated by statute . Where the deposit is made by a person
other than the accused , the relationship between principal and depositor
is entirely different from that between bail principal and surety . " Unlike
the right of a surety on a bail bond or recognizance to surrender the
principal in exoneration or discharge of their liability thereon , the fact
that a deposit in lieu of bail was made by a third person has been held not
to give him the right to take the body of the accused to the court or jailer . " 44

entries.ocr_text:
Kentucky is one of the states which provide by law for the deposit
of money in lieu of bail . 45 After a defendant has been admitted to bail in
a specified sum , he may deposit this sum with the court , and upon a certifi-
cate of such deposit being delivered to the officer having custody of de-
fendant , the accused will be released . After bail has been taken , a deposit
made in like manner will exonerate bail . The deposit must be returned if46the defendant surrender himself at any time before forfeiture . Should
a judgment be rendered against defendant for a fine or costs , the court
may order any money still on deposit to apply on same . Deposits of money
are subject to forfeiture on failure of defendant to appear as any other type
of bail .
Recognizance
Recognizance and bail are related terms and are often referred to
in the same connection . While the recognizance was originally a common
law obligation , it has been incorporated into the statutory law of most of the
states as well as in the federal rules and statutes . It has been defined thus :
In criminal cases , a ' bail bond ' is a contract under
seal , executed by accused , and from its nature requiring
sureties or bail , to whose custody he is committed ,
while a ' recognizance ' is an obligation of record entered
into before some court or magistrate authorized to take
it with condition to do some particular act , and a prisoner
is often allowed so to obligate himself to answer to the
charge.47
In other terms : " Aside from certain requisites as to form and
execution , it has been regarded as different from a bail bond merely in
the nature of the obligation created , the former being an acknowledgement
of record of an existing debt , the latter creating a new obligation . " 43
The early use of the recognizance in Kentucky reflects its common
law origin . Throughout this period the term " recognizance " was synono-
mous with " bail , " and the usage was the same . It appears that after the
adoption of the present constitution in 1891 the term " bail " came into
general use , the " recognizance " being reserved for a more limited appli-

witnesses in examining court to appear for the trial of the case , 49 and for

LIABILITY OF BAIL
Discharge and Exoneration
Though the terms are often used interchangeably in relation to
bail , technically there is a difference between " discharge " and " exonera-
tion " . Discharge is an official act of the court ; exoneration is the legal
cause upon which the discharge is based .
In general , bail is exonerated and discharged upon the appearance
of the accused to stand trial , or upon his surrender to the custody of the
sheriff or the court . However , this is not always the case . There may
be some special feature in the bond or provision in the law which allows
some qualification . For example , a form of bond known as a continuing
bond has been recognized , which requires the defendant to appear from
day to day and from term to term , and to abide the order of the court until
the cause is determined , or not to depart without leave , or both . This
type of bond does not release the surety where a prisoner escapes during
the trial of the case . The provision in the bond " not to depart without
leave " seems to be in general use in the federal courts .
Under Kentucky law , the defendant may surrender himself or be
surrendered to the jailer at any time before forfeiture and the money
deposited will be returned or the bail will be exonerated . However , where
there is a bond , there must be produced to the jailer a certified copy of
the bail bond , and obtained in return as a receipt therefor the written
acknowledgement of the jailer of the surrender of the defendant . The bail
bond thus operates as a commitment directed to the jailer.59 In similar
manner , the bail may arrest the defendant , or cause him to be arrested
by a peace officer or by any other person over twenty - one years who is
designated by an indorsement60 upon a certified copy of the bail bond ,
directing the arrest . A sheriff thus arresting a defendant upon authority
of his bail may not take a new bail . 61 Such arrest will exonerate the bail ,
as will a deposit of money after bail has been taken .
62
Enforced military service exonerates bail in most jurisdictions .
This has been written into the federal law as far as federal court appear-
ance is involved . In some states the rule is different when a voluntary
enlistment is involved .

the venue is changed as in the court originally contemplated by the

motion for change of venue . When a case is transferred from a state
court to a federal court , bail is exonerated in the state court and forfeiture
cannot be sustained in the state court . 63 Bail is also exonerated where
the state makes it impossible for accused to appear , as where he is held
by the state on another charge .
It seems to be a general rule that a final discharge of defendant
by the court discharges the bail , since there is no longer any need of
keeping the defendant in custody . Under this rule , it has been held that
the sureties cannot be held liable for non - appearance of the accused upon
being called back at a subsequent date . Moreover , a subsequent reversal
of the judgment discharging the defendant does not revive the surety's
liability .
The rule applying to the different federal districts is summarized

The view that the detention of the principal by another

been applied in the case of a principal released under
bail in one federal district and subsequently arrested
for a crime committed in another . In support of this
rule , it has been declared that the courts of different
federal districts are of the same jurisdiction .

the amount is adequate , and the security sufficient . However , such is
not the case , as actions on forfeiture are both numerous and complex .
Forfeiture of bail provisions are contained in the criminal code .

In determining whether the obligations of the bond

A bail bond or recognizance may impose distinct and in-

detained by federal court on a charge of crime ; or ( 5 ) serving in U.S.
Armed Forces . Very little latitude is allowed to the court on ( 1 ) . The
bail will be forfeited or a conditional forfeiture ordered , pending bona

The proceedings involved in the collection of forfeitures are not

The most important element in the defective ad-
ministration of forfeited bail is the carelessness shown
by the courts in the enforcement of forfeited bonds .
Many courts are extremely lax in the prosecution of
forfeited bonds with the natural result that the individual
surety makes no effort to aid the court in securing the
presence of the accused . Several instances of this
gross neglect in collection procedure are evidenced by
the practice of courts in widely spread jurisdictions . A
survey made in one Georgia County for a period of one
year related the fact that only $ 5,929 was ultimately
collected out of a total of $ 101,750 of forfeited bail bonds .
In the city of Atlanta , for the same period , only $ 15,462
was actually obtained from a sum of $ 100,000 of forfeited
bonds . Even worse examples can be found in other parts
of the nation . Chicago , for instance , has a $ 5,000 col-
lection from a total of $ 1,000,000 bail bonds , and a
Cleveland survey indicated only two percent of the for-
feited bonds were collected in that city . In 1950 only
20 percent of forfeitures were collected in Philadelphia ,
a higher rate of collection than the previous examples ,
but clearly inadequate to secure the purposes of the bail
system . These statistics indicate that courts , in the
past and present , have completely failed in their duty
to make effective the collection of bail . There is some
indication that a few courts are awakening to their
responsibility in collection matters , but it is believed
that until many more courts assume their duty in en-
forcing bail forfeiture , the administration of bail can
never be effective . 74
Statistics are not immediately available to indicate how the per

Disposition of Forfeitures
The disposition of money collected in forfeiture cases is made
in several ways . The Commonwealth's Attorney receives forty per cent
of all fines and forfeitures by the county judge or magistrates , and all
such in circuit court except the parts allowed the circuit clerk and county
attorney , up to the sum of $ 4,500 . Any surplus goes to the State Treasury.7
A circuit clerk receives five per cent of all forfeitures sent to the
State Treasury.76 The county attorney receives forty per cent of all
forfeitures prosecuted before the county judge , or magistrate , twenty-
five per cent recovered in circuit court where he is present and prosecutes ,
and thirty per cent of those recovered in police courts when he is present
77and prosecutes .
The court is given authority to dispose of cash deposited as bail
as seems best . 78 It may direct that it be forfeited upon non - compliance ,
but in any case the deposit exonerates bail . The deposit , or whatever part
that is necessary , may be applied by the court toward the payment of any
judgment that is rendered .
The issuance of a summons is the initiatory step in prosecuting a
forfeiture , although this is but the authorization of the start of necessary
procedure , since after such summons the matter proceeds under the
practice of the civil code , as in other cases involving debt . The duty is
then placed upon the attorney for the state , and there is nothing mandatory
upon him to prosecute the matter , the code providing that he " may" take
action . 79 The forfeitures collected have the same status as fines , the
statutes almost always using the words " fines and forfeitures " together .
The compensation is a percentage of the amount collected in all cases ,
whether it be the Commonwealth's Attorney , county attorney , or city
attorney , who initiates the proceedings .
75

ant in situations where bail later becomes insufficient and the matter is
properly called to their attention . The matter of bail is not inflexibly fixed

80The criminal code prescribes certain situations in which bail may
be changed , after admission to bail . A court in which a prosecution is
pending shall , by order , direct a defendant to be arrested and committed
to jail , after having given bail or deposited money in lieu thereof , in three
types of prosecutions : ( 1 ) when by having failed to appear , a forfeiture of
bail or of the money deposited has been incurred ; ( 2 ) when the court is
satisfied that his bail , or either of them , is dead or insufficient , or has
removed from the state ; and ( 3 ) upon an indictment being found for an of-
fense not bailable .
Under most of the circumstances the court could not have personal
knowledge of the facts , and a duty would seem to devolve upon the attorney
for the Commonwealth to advise the court . The court's power is summary
and a hearing is not provided for , although it seems to be indicate in the
first two situations , since the court again fixes the amount and conditions
of a new bail if it be offered . The amount of the later bond is named in the
process for his arrest .
KENTUCKY'S MULTIPLE BAIL SYSTEM
The incidence of admission to bail in Kentucky presents many
problems which are peculiar to the Commonwealth . There are provisions
for the taking of bail after each hearing , examination or trial , and at each
stage of prosecution a different bond may be required . While the general
code definition of the taking of bail81 appears simple , seeming to give the
court or officer taking bail a wide discretion , such is not the case . Other
sections of the code seem to limit or regulate the apparent power of the
court , thus resulting usually in each court requiring the execution of new
and separate bail bonds with the resulting inconvenience both to the defend-
ant and the state .
In the first instance , bail may be taken upon arrest before the
82magistrate issuing the warrant , or before the county judge . This is
applicable to all bailable offenses . If the arrest is without a warrant ,
the defendant is taken before the most convenient magistrate in the county .
If the offense is in a different county from the arrest , the magistrate may
transfer the defendant to the other county . 83

84A magistrate may take bail as an examining court . During ad-
journment of examining court , bail may be taken if the offense is bailable .
After the examination , the magistrate may discharge defendant on bail.86
If a trial in a magistrate's court is postponed , defendant may make bail.87
Then , after commitment , bail may be fixed by the committing magistrate
88or judge of the county court .
After an indictment has been found , if the offense be bailable , bail
is again fixed by the court . There is no provision for continuing any pre-
viously fixed or made bail.89 If the bench warrant be served by one other
than the sheriff , such other officer , upon request by the defendant , shall
take him before the sheriff for the purpose of fixing bail.90
85
Upon the trial of a felony , the defendant , if on bail , may remain on
bail during the trial.91 If the case be a misdemeanor , the same rule applies
but defendant need not be present to be tried . 92 After bail has been given to
appear in trial court , the court may , in enumerated cases , void the bail
and recommit defendant , who may then make another bail.93
After conviction , the defendant may make bond except for specific
crimes designated in the code . 94 This bond must be made in the trial court
before the record is filed in the Court of Appeals upon appeal . After appeal
and filing the record in the Court of Appeals , bond must be made there .
These provisions in the code requiring so many different bonds
are in some degree necessary because of the number of courts in which
a defendant may be required to appear . In some cases the same bond
may be continued from examining trial to final conviction , but in any
case the surety must appear to make new bail or be recognized to be bound
on the original , in the discretion of the court . If the sureties live in re-
mote sections of the county , or in another county , this can be burdensome .
One of the by - products of this multiplicity of bail is the rise of professional
bondsmen , especially in metropolitan centers . With a ten per cent average
for a bondsman's fee , this multiple bond situation does not seem to be
in the best interests of the cause of justice . The single bond once made ,
as generally used in the federal system95 has evidently worked better ,
subject to cooperation by bondsmen ; and at the same time is more reason-
able both to the defendant and the prosecution .

CODE SECTION EXAMINATION
BAIL AND RECOGNIZANCE
General Comment
Present Law
The base of the enacted law of bail is in sections 16 and 17 of the
Kentucky constitution , guarantying bail for all except capital offenses where
" the proof is evident or the presumption great " . Nearly all of the rest of
the law of bail is in the criminal code , which is rather complete as to pro-
cedures for bail in criminal cases . Civil bail provisions are contained in
the statutes , as are miscellaneous criminal bail provisions , including
provisions as to police courts bail powers ( KRS 26.370 to 26.390 ) ; impri-
sonment in default of ( KRS 431.150 ) ; false impersonation for (KRS 434.060 ) ;
denial to persons imprisoned for contempt ( KRS 432.270 ) ; and others . The
various aspects of bail are discussed fully in the first part of this publica-
tion .
General Revision of Sections
The general treatment of bail provisions by this proposed revision
is basically the same as that in the present code . Unnecessary sections
are omitted , key sections are revised , and new sections are added , but the
general arrangement and scope is unchanged . Provisions as to recognizance
are retained in language which encourages use of the term , " bail " , where
surety is required . Some provisions are broadened to encompass terms of
peace bonds where desired . These revisions are indicated in more detail
in the section examination which follows .
Effect of Revision on Existing Law
The revision of key sections would strengthen these provisions by
making them simpler and more practical . The qualifications for bail are
broadened but at the same time the justification required is more complete .
The surrender provisions are brought into harmony with the modern law
of arrest ; deposit of money provisions are broadened to include debentures ,
but limited in applicability to fines and costs ; and the forfeiture provisions
are simplified and made more effective . Forfeitures are made more en-
forceable because some would be collectible in inferior courts , not just in
circuit court as in the present code .
New proposals codify the burden of proof on application for bail
in capital offenses and set standards for determining the amount of bail .
Another proposal encourages the continuation of a single bail bond through-
out all pre - conviction proceedings .

APPENDIX
Related Statutes : Disposition of Forfeiture ( Cr . C. 97 ) .
The statutes require that the clerks of circuit courts collect
and transmit all fines and forfeitures imposed for the benefit of the
State , and that the excess from county courts be returned to the county
from which sent . There is a duty on the clerks of all courts to pay over
to the circuit clerks all such fines and forfeitures at the close of each
term . (KRS 28.170 ; 431. 100 ; 431. 110 and 46.050 )
KRS 28.170 Collection of fines and forfeitures . The circuit
clerk in each county shall attend to the collection of all fines and for-
feitures , The clerk of each court having jurisdiction to impose fines in
favor of the state shall , at the close of each term , report to the circuit
clerk a list of all fines and forfeitures imposed in his court at such term ,
and shall pay all fines and forfeitures collected to the circuit clerk who
shall forward them to the State Treasury .
KRS 431. 100 Fines and forfeitures go to state ; exceptions ;
how recovered . All fines and forfeitures imposed by law shall inure
to and vest in the state , except in cases where , by law , the whole or
a part thereof is given to a person or to some particular object , and
may be recovered by civil procedure before any court having juris-
diction or upon indictment of a grand jury .
KRS 431. 110 Fines and forfeitures from county and quarter-
ly courts returned to county . Except as otherwise provided by law , all
fines and forfeitures imposed by the county judge of each county and
paid into the State Treasury through the circuit clerk shall , after de-
ducting the part allowed by law to officers , be paid to the county from
which sent .
KRS 46.050 Fines and forfeitures collected by circuit clerk ,
how paid in and accounted for . The fines and forfeitures collected by
the circuit clerk under KRS 28.170 shall be paid into the State Treasury
and accounted for in the manner provided by law for state tax moneys .
